graph_vector_fulltext.message:
The Regulation treats “AI models” as components that can be embedded or integrated into “AI systems”: AI models “do not constitute AI systems,” while AI systems are built on or integrate AI models and can be placed on the market or put into service. It specifically recognises “general-purpose AI models” as a subtype of AI models, characterised by significant generality and capability to perform a wide range of distinctive tasks, and which can form the basis for downstream systems.

Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models apply once those models are “placed on the market.” If a provider integrates its own model into its own AI system that is made available on the market or put into service, that model is considered placed on the market, so the model obligations continue to apply in addition to the AI system obligations.

From the provided excerpt, the main situation where the obligations for general-purpose AI models do not apply is before the model is placed on the market (i.e., prior to being “placed on the market,” the model obligations are not yet triggered). The excerpt also indicates that general-purpose AI models have an “exception from transparency-related requirements,” but it does not specify in this excerpt the full conditions or scope of that exception.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: How does the Regulation distinguish between 'general-purpose AI models' and 'AI systems', and when do the obligations for general-purpose AI models NOT apply?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
The Regulation treats “AI models” as components that can be embedded or integrated into “AI systems”: AI models “do not constitute AI systems,” while AI systems are built on or integrate AI models and can be placed on the market or put into service. It specifically recognises “general-purpose AI models” as a subtype of AI models, characterised by significant generality and capability to perform a wide range of distinctive tasks, and which can form the basis for downstream systems.

Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models apply once those models are “placed on the market.” If a provider integrates its own model into its own AI system that is made available on the market or put into service, that model is considered placed on the market, so the model obligations continue to apply in addition to the AI system obligations.

From the provided excerpt, the main situation where the obligations for general-purpose AI models do not apply is before the model is placed on the market (i.e., prior to being “placed on the market,” the model obligations are not yet triggered). The excerpt also indicates that general-purpose AI models have an “exception from transparency-related requirements,” but it does not specify in this excerpt the full conditions or scope of that exception.

Content: Text Content:
published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European  harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI  models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice  or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant  market actors that are placing them
----
 be understood that the obligations for the providers of general-purpose AI  models should apply once the general-purpose AI models are placed on the market. When the provider of  a general-purpose AI model integrates an own model into its own AI system that is made available on the market or  put into service, that model should be considered to be placed on the market and, therefore, the obligations in this  Regulation for models should continue to apply in addition to those for AI systems. The obligations
----
 the obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models should be a competence of the  Commission. The AI Office should be able to carry out all necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation  of this Regulation as regards general-purpose AI models. It should be able to investigate possible infringements of  the rules on providers of general-purpose AI models both on its own initiative, following the results of its  monitoring activities, or upon request from market surveillance authorities in line with the conditions set out in this
----
 should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and  compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI  Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this  Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by  requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models. When conducting evaluations, in order to make  use of independent expertise, the

chunks.text: be understood that the obligations for the providers of general-purpose AI models should apply once the general-purpose AI models are placed on the market. When the provider of a general-purpose AI model integrates an own model into its own AI system that is made available on the market or put into service, that model should be considered to be placed on the market and, therefore, the obligations in this Regulation for models should continue to apply in addition to those for AI systems. The obligations

published and assessed as suitable to cover the relevant obligations by the AI Office, compliance with a European harmonised standard should grant providers the presumption of conformity. Providers of general-purpose AI models should furthermore be able to demonstrate compliance using alternative adequate means, if codes of practice or harmonised standards are not available, or they choose not to rely on those. (118) This Regulation regulates AI systems and AI models by imposing certain requirements and obligations for relevant market actors that are placing them

the obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models should be a competence of the Commission. The AI Office should be able to carry out all necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of this Regulation as regards general-purpose AI models. It should be able to investigate possible infringements of the rules on providers of general-purpose AI models both on its own initiative, following the results of its monitoring activities, or upon request from market surveillance authorities in line with the conditions set out in this

should be able to take the necessary actions to monitor the effective implementation of and compliance with the obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models laid down in this Regulation. The AI Office should be able to investigate possible infringements in accordance with the powers provided for in this Regulation, including by requesting documentation and information, by conducting evaluations, as well as by requesting measures from providers of general-purpose AI models.
